info.name: Tractus-X EDC — Management API (Local DCP)
info.description:
# Tractus-X EDC Management API — Local DCP Deployment

Comprehensive collection covering the **full EDC Management API v3** for a local DCP (Decentralized Claims Protocol) deployment with per-company IdentityHub wallets.

## Architecture

| Component | Host Port | Docker Name |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|
| Provider Control Plane | 19191-19195 | provider-cp |
| Provider Data Plane | 19196-19198 | provider-dp |
| Consumer Control Plane | 29191-29195 | consumer-cp |
| Consumer Data Plane | 29196-29198 | consumer-dp |
| Provider IdentityHub | 7181/7292 | provider-ih |
| Consumer IdentityHub | 8182/8293 | consumer-ih |
| BDRS Server | 8580/8581 | bdrs-server |
| Issuer Service | 18181/18292 | issuerservice |

## How to Use

### Quick Start (Run All)
1. Right-click the collection → **Run collection**
2. All requests execute in order — test scripts chain variables automatically.
3. The flow: Create assets → policies → contract defs → browse catalog → negotiate → transfer → pull data → cleanup.

### Step-by-Step
1. Run **1. Health Checks** to verify deployment.
2. Run **2. Asset Management** → CRUD operations on assets.
3. Run **3. Policy Definitions** → CRUD + validate + evaluation plan.
4. Run **4. Contract Definitions** → CRUD operations.
5. Run **5. Catalog Browsing** → full catalog, filtered catalog, dataset request.
6. Run **6. Contract Negotiation** → initiate, poll, query, terminate.
7. Run **7. Contract Agreements** → inspect agreements + trace back to negotiations.
8. Run **8. Transfer Process** → initiate HttpData-PULL, poll, query, get state.
9. Run **9. EDR & Data Pull** → get EDR, pull data, refresh token, delete EDR.
10. Run **10. PUSH Transfer** → push-based data delivery.
11. Run **11. EDR Cache** → initiate EDR negotiation, query by asset/agreement.
12. Run **12. Transfer Lifecycle** → suspend/resume/terminate/deprovision.
13. Run **14. BPN Groups** (optional) → Tractus-X BPN-to-group mappings.
14. Run **15. Agreement Retirements** (optional) → retire/reactivate agreements.
15. Run **16. Connector Discovery** (optional) → v4alpha endpoint discovery.
16. Run **17. Cleanup** → remove demo resources.

## Endpoint Coverage & API Taxonomy

This collection covers **65 requests** across the full EDC Management API v3 + Tractus-X extensions.
Understanding which endpoints come from upstream EDC vs Tractus-X is critical for troubleshooting,
upgrades, and understanding the Catena-X architecture.

### Pure Upstream EDC Endpoints (Used As-Is)

These endpoints come directly from the [Eclipse EDC](https://github.com/eclipse-edc/Connector)
project with **zero modification** by Tractus-X. The CRUD operations, request/response formats,
and behavior are identical to vanilla EDC:

| Folder | API Path | Endpoints | Purpose |
|--------|----------|-----------|---------|
| 2. Assets | `/v3/assets` | 5 (CRUD + query) | Register data offerings with metadata and data addresses |
| 3. Policies | `/v3/policydefinitions` | 7 (CRUD + query + validate + eval plan) | Define ODRL access & contract policies |
| 4. Contract Defs | `/v3/contractdefinitions` | 4 (CRUD + query) | Link assets to policies — makes them negotiable |
| 5. Catalog | `/v3/catalog/request`, `/v3/catalog/dataset/request` | 3 | Browse provider offerings via DSP protocol |
| 6. Negotiations | `/v3/contractnegotiations` | 6 (initiate + poll + query + state + agreement + terminate) | Consumer-initiated contract negotiation |
| 7. Agreements | `/v3/contractagreements` | 3 (get + query + negotiation) | Inspect finalized agreements |
| 8. Transfers | `/v3/transferprocesses` | 4 (initiate + poll + query + state) | Initiate and track data transfers |
| 12. Lifecycle | `/v3/transferprocesses/{id}/suspend\|resume\|terminate\|deprovision` | 4 | Transfer state management |

> **Why unmodified?** These are the standard Dataspace Protocol (DSP) building blocks.
> Tractus-X adds value through *policy functions* and *extensions*, not by changing core CRUD.

### TX-Customized Endpoints (Extended Upstream Behavior)

These use upstream EDC API paths but Tractus-X **replaces or extends** the underlying implementation:

| Folder | API Path | What TX Changes | Why |
|--------|----------|-----------------|-----|
| 9. EDR & Data Pull | `/v3/edrs/{id}/dataaddress` | **Auto-refresh** (`?auto_refresh=true`) — TX's `edr-api-v2` replaces upstream `edr-cache-api`. Checks token expiry and renews automatically. | Long-running data exchanges need token refresh without re-negotiation. Upstream EDC returns stale tokens. |
| 9. EDR & Data Pull | `/v3/edrs/{id}/refresh` | **Force refresh** — explicitly request a new EDR token. Not in upstream EDC. | Consumers need on-demand token renewal for time-sensitive operations. |
| 9. EDR & Data Pull | `DELETE /v3/edrs/{id}` | **EDR deletion** — remove cached EDR entries. | Cache hygiene for completed transfers. |
| 11. EDR Cache | `POST /v3/edrs` | **EDR Negotiation Initiation** — single call that triggers contract negotiation + automatic EDR retrieval. Not in upstream. | Simplifies the 3-step flow (negotiate → transfer → get EDR) into one call for common use cases. |
| 11. EDR Cache | `POST /v3/edrs/request` | **EDR query with filters** — query cached EDRs by asset ID, agreement ID, etc. | Enables cache inspection and management across multiple active data exchanges. |

> **Why customize EDRs?** Upstream EDC's EDR cache API is minimal — it stores tokens but doesn't
> handle refresh, auto-renewal, or single-call negotiation. In production Catena-X deployments,
> data exchanges run for hours/days, making token refresh essential.

**CX Policy Engine Extensions** (invisible at the API level, but critical):

Tractus-X registers **25+ custom ODRL constraint functions** via `edc-extensions/cx-policy/` that
execute during catalog requests, negotiations, and transfers. These don't add new endpoints but
fundamentally change how policies are evaluated:

| Left Operand | Constraint Function | Purpose |
|--------------|-------------------|---------|
| `cx-policy:FrameworkAgreement` | `FrameworkAgreementConstraintFunction` | Require specific CX framework agreement (e.g., `DataExchangeGovernance:1.0`) |
| `cx-policy:UsagePurpose` | `UsagePurposeConstraintFunction` | Restrict data usage to specific purposes (e.g., `cx.core.industrycore:1`) |
| `cx-policy:BusinessPartnerNumber` | `BusinessPartnerNumberConstraintFunction` | Restrict access to specific BPNLs |
| `cx-policy:BusinessPartnerGroup` | `BusinessPartnerGroupConstraintFunction` | Restrict access by BPN group membership (uses folder 14's API) |
| `cx-policy:Membership` | `MembershipCredentialConstraintFunction` | Require valid CX membership credential |
| `cx-policy:Dismantler` | `DismantlerCredentialConstraintFunction` | Require dismantler credential |
| `cx-policy:UsageRestriction` | `UsageRestrictionConstraintFunction` | General usage restrictions |
| `cx-policy:DataUsageEndDate` | `DataUsageEndDateConstraintFunction` | Time-bound data access |
| ... | *(15+ more)* | Warranty, liability, jurisdiction, confidentiality, etc. |

> **Why custom policies?** Upstream EDC ships with a generic policy engine that understands ODRL
> syntax but has no domain knowledge. Catena-X needs automotive-specific constraints
> (framework agreements, dismantler credentials, usage purposes) that map to Verifiable
> Credentials in the participant's Identity Hub wallet.

### TX-Added Endpoints (Entirely New — Not in Upstream EDC)

These endpoints are **entirely new** — created by Tractus-X extensions to solve Catena-X-specific
problems that the upstream EDC doesn't address:

| Folder | API Path | Endpoints | Extension Module | Why It Exists |
|--------|----------|-----------|-----------------|---------------|
| 14. BPN Groups | `/v3/business-partner-groups` | 6 (CRUD + query by BPN/group) | `edc-extensions/bpn-validation` | **Group-based access control.** Catena-X has thousands of participants. Instead of listing every BPNL in a policy, operators create groups (e.g., "gold-partners", "tier-1-suppliers") and use `BusinessPartnerGroup` constraints. This API manages the BPN↔group mappings that the policy engine evaluates. |
| 15. Agreement Retirements | `/v3/contractagreements/retirements` | 3 (retire + query + reactivate) | `edc-extensions/agreements` | **Compliance lifecycle.** Upstream EDC has no concept of "retiring" an agreement — once finalized, it's permanent. In Catena-X, business relationships change: a supplier may lose certification, or a framework agreement may expire. Retirement makes agreements inactive for new transfers while preserving them in the audit trail. |
| 16. Connector Discovery | `/v4alpha/connectordiscovery` | 2 (DSP versions + discover by BPN) | `edc-extensions/connector-discovery` | **Multi-connector resolution.** In Catena-X, one organization may operate multiple connectors. Given a participant's DID or BPN, this API resolves which connector endpoints are available and what DSP protocol versions they support. Uses BDRS (BPN Directory Resolution Service) for DID↔BPN mapping. |
| *(not in collection)* | `/v4alpha/dataflows/{id}/trigger` | 1 | `edc-extensions/dataplane/dataflow` | **On-demand data push.** Manually trigger a data flow execution — used for event-driven or scheduled push scenarios where the standard transfer lifecycle is too automated. |

> **Why new endpoints?** Upstream EDC is a generic dataspace connector. Catena-X is an automotive
> industry dataspace with specific governance: BPN-based identity, framework agreements,
> credential-based trust, and compliance requirements that need purpose-built APIs.

### TX Data Plane Extensions (Not Management API)

These TX extensions add endpoints on the **Data Plane** (not the Management API), so they're not
in this Postman collection but are part of the Tractus-X architecture:

| API Path | Context | Extension Module | Purpose |
|----------|---------|-----------------|---------|
| `/token` | Public (Data Plane) | `dataplane-token-refresh` | OAuth2-compatible token refresh for active EDRs |
| `/aas/request` | Proxy | `dataplane-proxy-consumer-api` | Direct asset fetch bypassing Management API (for consumer apps) |
| `/{any:.*}` (public API v2) | Public | `dataplane-public-api-v2` | Enhanced public data plane with TX-specific auth |

### Supporting TX Extensions (No New Endpoints)

These don't expose API endpoints but modify runtime behavior:

| Module | What It Does | Why |
|--------|-------------|-----|
| `bdrs-client` | Resolves DID ↔ BPN mappings via BDRS server | Every DSP interaction needs BPN↔DID translation |
| `tokenrefresh-handler` | Background EDR token refresh service | Keeps long-running transfer tokens alive |
| `provision-additional-headers` | Injects BPNL headers into outgoing HTTP requests | Data providers need to know who's requesting data |
| `data-flow-properties-provider` | Enriches data flows with participant context | Downstream systems need org identity metadata |
| `token-interceptor` | Token injection at HTTP client level | All connector-to-connector calls need auth tokens |
| `validators` | Blocks empty asset selectors in contract definitions | Prevents accidental exposure of all assets |
| `agreements-bpns` | Stores BPNL associations on negotiation events | Audit trail: which BPN negotiated which agreement |
| `dcp/` | W3C VC caching + DIV integration | Performance: cache validated credentials instead of re-verifying every request |
| `backport/` | Upstream EDC bug fixes applied locally | Fixes that haven't yet been merged upstream |

## Why Some Tests Accept Non-2xx Responses

Out of 65+ requests, only **2** accept non-2xx responses as passing tests:

| Request | Accepted Codes | Reason |
|---------|---------------|--------|
| **Delete EDR** (9. EDR & Data Pull) | 200, 204, 404 | EDR may have expired or been previously deleted. Idempotent cleanup pattern. |
| **Delete Asset** (17. Cleanup) | 204, 409 | EDC referential integrity prevents deletion when active agreements reference the asset. Agreements are immutable audit records — this is correct behavior. |

All other requests expect **strict 2xx responses**. Earlier versions of this collection had tolerant assertions for more endpoints, but these were systematically fixed by:
- Adding `policyScope` to Evaluation Plan requests
- Using a \"sacrificial negotiation\" pattern for Terminate Negotiation (pre-request initiates a fresh negotiation, then terminates it before finalization)
- Switching suspend/resume to PUSH transfers (PULL transfers don't support suspend)
- Targeting TERMINATED negotiations for deletion instead of FINALIZED ones
- `DataService` endpoints are now auto-registered by connector self-registration (previously manual in bootstrap Step 8)

## JSON-LD Gotchas

> These were discovered through extensive debugging and are baked into every request:

- **`odrl:action`** must be `{"@id": "odrl:use"}` — bare `"use"` fails validation.
- **`odrl:assigner`** in negotiations must be the **BPN** (e.g., `BPNL000000000001`), not the DID.
- **`odrl:leftOperand`** for CX policies must use full IRIs: `{"@id": "https://w3id.org/catenax/2025/9/policy/FrameworkAgreement"}`.
- **`odrl:target`** must match the asset `@id` from the catalog exactly.
- **`@id` (Offer ID)** in negotiation must match the catalog's `odrl:hasPolicy.@id` exactly.

## Variables

All configuration is in **Collection Variables** — edit them to match your deployment.

## Running with Newman

```bash
newman run EDC_Management_API_DCP.postman_collection.json --delay-request 2000
```

The `--delay-request 2000` flag adds a 2-second delay between requests, which is important for polling loops (negotiation status, transfer status) that use `postman.setNextRequest()` retries.

## Pre-request Guards

Dependent requests (negotiation, transfer, EDR) include **pre-request guard scripts** that check if required variables are set. If you run a request out of order, you'll get a clear error message telling you which step to run first.

exec: // Collection-level pre-request: generate unique suffix for demo resources

item.name: 1. Health Checks
item.description:
Verify all EDC services are running and healthy before proceeding.

This collection targets a **local DCP deployment** with 4 services:
- **Provider CP** (Control Plane) — `localhost:19191` — orchestrates catalog, negotiation, and transfer
- **Provider DP** (Data Plane) — `localhost:19196` — handles data transfer (push/pull)
- **Consumer CP** — `localhost:29191` — the data consumer's control plane
- **Consumer DP** — `localhost:29196` — the consumer's data plane

All should return `{"isSystemHealthy": true}` with HTTP 200.

> **If any health check fails**, the downstream API calls will also fail. Ensure all Docker containers are running.

request.description:
Check Provider Control Plane health.

Expected: HTTP 200 with `{"isSystemHealthy": true}`.

**GET** `/check/health`

Check Provider Data Plane health.

The Data Plane is responsible for the actual data transfer — both PULL (serving data via EDR) and PUSH (posting data to consumer-provided endpoints).

Expected: HTTP 200 with `{"isSystemHealthy": true}`.

**GET** `/check/health`

Check Consumer Control Plane health.

The Consumer CP orchestrates catalog browsing, contract negotiation, and transfer initiation — all on behalf of the data consumer.

Expected: HTTP 200 with `{"isSystemHealthy": true}`.

**GET** `/check/health`

Check Consumer Data Plane health.

The Consumer DP is present for symmetry. For PUSH transfers, the consumer only needs an HTTP endpoint (not a Data Plane) — the provider's DP pushes data there directly.

Expected: HTTP 200 with `{"isSystemHealthy": true}`.

item.name: 2. Asset Management (Provider)
item.description:
**Origin: Upstream EDC** — standard asset management, used as-is.

Create assets on the **provider** side. Assets represent the data offerings.

Each asset has:
- **properties**: metadata (name, type, description)
- **dataAddress**: where the actual data lives (HttpData URL, S3 bucket, etc.)

The `@id` is the unique asset identifier used throughout the dataspace.

request.description:
**POST** `/v3/assets`

Create a new asset on the provider. The asset wraps:
- `properties.name` — human-readable name
- `properties.dct:type` — asset taxonomy type
- `dataAddress.baseUrl` — the actual data source URL

The `@id` is auto-generated as `demo-asset-<timestamp>` by the collection pre-request script.

**Response**: `IdResponse` with the created asset's `@id` and `createdAt` timestamp.

**GET** `/v3/assets/{id}`

Retrieve a single asset by its ID.

**Response**: Full Asset object with `properties`, `dataAddress`, and metadata.

**POST** `/v3/assets/request`

Query all assets on the provider using a QuerySpec.

- `offset` / `limit` — pagination
- `sortField` / `sortOrder` — optional sorting
- `filterExpression` — optional filtering (see next request)

**Response**: Array of Asset objects.

**POST** `/v3/assets/request` with `filterExpression`

Query a specific asset by its ID.

> **Note**: The `operandLeft` must be the full namespace IRI `https://w3id.org/edc/v0.0.1/ns/id`, not just `id`.

**Response**: Array containing the matching asset (or empty array).

**PUT** `/v3/assets`

Update an existing asset. The `@id` in the body identifies which asset to update. All fields are replaced.

**Response**: 204 No Content on success.

item.name: 3. Policy Definitions (Provider)
item.description:
**Origin: Upstream EDC** — standard policy CRUD. Tractus-X adds 25+ custom constraint functions (FrameworkAgreement, UsagePurpose, BusinessPartnerGroup, etc.) that execute when these policies are evaluated, but the API itself is unchanged.

Create ODRL policy definitions on the **provider**.

Two types:
- **Access Policy** — controls who can *see* the asset in the catalog (visibility)
- **Contract Policy** — controls the constraints for *negotiating* a contract

## CX Policy Constraints
Catena-X uses custom left operands under `https://w3id.org/catenax/2025/9/policy/`:
- `FrameworkAgreement` — which framework the consumer agreed to
- `UsagePurpose` — allowed usage purposes
- `Membership` — active CX membership

## JSON-LD Gotcha
`odrl:action` MUST be `{"@id": "odrl:use"}` — a bare string `"use"` causes a 400 validation error.

request.description:
**POST** `/v3/policydefinitions`

Create a **contract policy** with Catena-X framework constraints:
- `FrameworkAgreement = DataExchangeGovernance:1.0`
- `UsagePurpose isAnyOf cx.core.industrycore:1`

These constraints require the consumer to hold matching Verifiable Credentials.

### JSON-LD Critical Rules
- `odrl:action` → `{"@id": "odrl:use"}` (not bare `"use"`)
- `odrl:leftOperand` → `{"@id": "cx-policy:FrameworkAgreement"}` (IRI object form)
- `odrl:operator` → `{"@id": "odrl:eq"}` (IRI object form)

**POST** `/v3/policydefinitions/request`

Query all policy definitions on the provider. Supports `QuerySpec` with pagination and filtering.

**GET** `/v3/policydefinitions/{id}`

Retrieve a single policy definition by its ID.

**Response**: Full PolicyDefinition object with ODRL policy content.

**PUT** `/v3/policydefinitions/{id}`

Update an existing policy definition. The entire policy body is replaced.

> Policies referenced by active contract definitions should not be modified.

**Response**: 204 No Content on success.

**POST** `/v3/policydefinitions/{id}/validate`

Validate a policy definition. Checks whether constraints can be evaluated by the installed policy engine.

Useful for debugging constraint mismatches.

**Response**: Validation result (200 OK if valid).

**POST** `/v3/policydefinitions/{id}/evaluationplan`

Get the evaluation plan for a policy. Shows which policy functions handle which operands.

Useful for debugging why negotiations fail.

The `policyScope` parameter tells the engine which evaluation context to use:
- `contract.negotiation` — functions registered for contract negotiation
- `catalog` — functions registered for catalog filtering
- `transfer.process` — functions registered for transfer validation

**Response**: Evaluation plan object showing constraint functions, pre/post validators.

item.name: 4. Contract Definitions (Provider)
item.description:
**Origin: Upstream EDC** — standard contract definition CRUD, used as-is.

Contract definitions **link** an access policy, a contract policy, and an asset selector together.

This is the final step to make an asset visible and negotiable in the dataspace:

```
Contract Definition
  ├── accessPolicyId    → who can SEE the asset
  ├── contractPolicyId  → what constraints apply to NEGOTIATE
  └── assetsSelector    → which assets this applies to
```

request.description:
**POST** `/v3/contractdefinitions`

Create a contract definition that binds:
- **accessPolicyId** → the unrestricted access policy
- **contractPolicyId** → the CX constraint policy
- **assetsSelector** → filters to the specific demo asset

Once created, the asset becomes visible in the provider's catalog to consumers who pass the access policy.

**GET** `/v3/contractdefinitions/{id}`

Retrieve a single contract definition by its ID.

**Response**: Full ContractDefinition with `accessPolicyId`, `contractPolicyId`, `assetsSelector`.

**POST** `/v3/contractdefinitions/request`

Query all contract definitions. Each result shows:
- `accessPolicyId` — the access gating policy
- `contractPolicyId` — the negotiation constraint policy
- `assetsSelector` — matching criteria

**PUT** `/v3/contractdefinitions`

Update an existing contract definition. The `@id` identifies which to update.

> Changing policies only affects future negotiations.

**Response**: 204 No Content on success.

item.name: 5. Catalog Browsing (Consumer → Provider)
item.description:
**Origin: Upstream EDC** — standard DSP catalog protocol, used as-is. The DCP authentication flow (DID resolution → IdentityHub → VP/VC validation) is added by Tractus-X but doesn't change the catalog API format.

The **consumer** requests the **provider's** catalog via DSP (Dataspace Protocol).

This triggers a full DCP authentication flow:
1. Consumer CP resolves provider DID via BDRS
2. Consumer requests a Self-Issued token from its IdentityHub
3. Provider validates the token and returns the catalog

The catalog contains `dcat:dataset` entries — each with `odrl:hasPolicy` offers that describe available terms.

request.description:
**POST** `/v3/catalog/request`

Fetch the full catalog from the provider.

- `counterPartyAddress` — provider's DSP endpoint (Docker-internal hostname)
- `counterPartyId` — provider's DID
- `protocol` — must be `dataspace-protocol-http`

**Response**: DCAT Catalog with `dcat:dataset` array. Each dataset has:
- `@id` — asset ID
- `odrl:hasPolicy` — array of ODRL offers with constraints

> This request exercises the full DCP flow (DID resolution → SI token → VP presentation).

**POST** `/v3/catalog/request` with `querySpec.filterExpression`

Fetch only the specific demo asset from the catalog.

The test script **automatically extracts** the `offerId` and `catalogAssetId` variables for use in the negotiation step.

exec: pm.test('Filtered catalog has our asset', function () {

request.description:
**POST** `/v3/catalog/dataset/request`

Request a specific dataset from the provider's catalog by asset ID.

Returns a single `dcat:Dataset` with all its `odrl:hasPolicy` offers.

**Response**: Single DCAT Dataset object.

exec: if (!aid) throw new Error('assetId not set. Run "2. Create Asset" first.');

item.name: 6. Contract Negotiation (Consumer → Provider)
item.description:
**Origin: Upstream EDC** — standard contract negotiation protocol, used as-is.

The **consumer** initiates a contract negotiation with the provider.

The negotiation payload must include an ODRL `Offer` that **exactly matches** the policy from the catalog — including:
- `@id` → the offer ID from the catalog
- `odrl:assigner` → the provider's **BPN** (not DID!)
- `odrl:target` → the asset ID
- All constraints with full IRI operands

The negotiation goes through states: `REQUESTED` → `AGREED` → `VERIFIED` → `FINALIZED`.

Once `FINALIZED`, a **contract agreement** is created on both sides.

request.description:
**POST** `/v3/contractnegotiations`

Initiate a contract negotiation.

### Critical JSON-LD Rules
| Field | Correct | Wrong |
|-------|---------|-------|
| `odrl:assigner` | `{"@id": "BPNL000000000001"}` | `{"@id": "did:web:..."}` |
| `odrl:action` | `{"@id": "odrl:use"}` | `"use"` |
| `odrl:leftOperand` | `{"@id": "https://...FrameworkAgreement"}` | `"FrameworkAgreement"` |
| `@id` (offer) | Exact catalog offer ID | Any other value |

> The `offerId` and `catalogAssetId` are auto-populated from the catalog browse step.

**Response**: `IdResponse` with the `negotiationId`.

exec: // Guard: ensure catalog variables are set
throw new Error('offerId or catalogAssetId not set. Run "5. Request Filtered Catalog" first.');

request.description:
**GET** `/v3/contractnegotiations/{id}`

Poll the negotiation status. States:
- `REQUESTED` — sent to provider, waiting
- `AGREED` — provider agreed
- `VERIFIED` — consumer verified
- **`FINALIZED`** — agreement created ✓
- `TERMINATED` — failed ✗

> **Auto-retries** up to 15 times when run via newman/collection runner.
> Use `--delay-request 2000` in Newman to add 2s between retries.
> The test script automatically extracts the `agreementId` when finalized.

exec: throw new Error('negotiationId not set. Run "6. Initiate Negotiation" first.');
// Add 2s delay between polls (DCP credential exchange needs time)
pm.test('Negotiation should not be TERMINATED', function () {
pm.test('Negotiation should finalize within 15 polls', function () {
pm.expect.fail('Timed out after 15 polls, last state: ' + state);

request.description:
**POST** `/v3/contractnegotiations/request`

Query all negotiations on the consumer. Supports `QuerySpec` with pagination and filtering.

**GET** `/v3/contractnegotiations/{id}/state`

Get only the state (lightweight alternative to full GET).

**Response**: `{"state": "FINALIZED"}`.

exec: if (!negId) throw new Error('negotiationId not set. Run "6. Initiate Negotiation" first.');

request.description:
**GET** `/v3/contractnegotiations/{id}/agreement`

Get the agreement produced by a finalized negotiation.

Shortcut: instead of extracting `contractAgreementId` and calling `GET /v3/contractagreements/{id}`.

**Response**: Full ContractAgreement object.

exec: if (!negId) throw new Error('negotiationId not set. Run "6. Initiate Negotiation" first.');

request.description:
**POST** `/v3/contractnegotiations/{id}/terminate`

Terminate an in-progress negotiation with a reason.

### How this works
The pre-request script initiates a **new** negotiation (using the same offer from the catalog) and immediately terminates it before it reaches FINALIZED. This demonstrates the terminate API on a genuinely in-progress negotiation.

> The main flow's negotiation (used for agreements and transfers) is preserved.

**Response**: 204 No Content.

exec: // Initiate a NEW negotiation so we can terminate it before it finalizes.
// The main negotiation (negotiationId) is already FINALIZED and cannot be terminated.
throw new Error('offerId or catalogAssetId not set. Run catalog browse first.');
if (err) throw new Error('Failed to initiate sacrificial negotiation: ' + err);
if (!newId) throw new Error('No @id in negotiation response: ' + JSON.stringify(body));

item.name: 7. Contract Agreements
item.description:
**Origin: Upstream EDC** — standard agreement queries, used as-is.

Once a negotiation is `FINALIZED`, a **contract agreement** exists on both consumer and provider.

The agreement contains:
- `assetId` — what was negotiated
- `providerId` / `consumerId` — parties (BPNs)
- `contractSigningDate` — epoch timestamp
- `policy` — the agreed-upon ODRL policy

request.description:
**GET** `/v3/contractagreements/{id}`

Retrieve a specific contract agreement by its ID.

> The `agreementId` is auto-populated from the negotiation step.

exec: throw new Error('agreementId not set. Run negotiation flow (folder 6) until FINALIZED first.');

request.description:
**POST** `/v3/contractagreements/request`

Query all contract agreements on the consumer side.

Supports the same `QuerySpec` with `filterExpression` for searching by `assetId`, `providerId`, etc.

**GET** `/v3/contractagreements/{id}/negotiation`

Get the negotiation that produced this agreement. Useful for tracing back.

**Response**: Full ContractNegotiation object.

item.name: 8. Transfer Process — HttpData-PULL
item.description:
**Origin: Upstream EDC** — standard transfer process management, used as-is.

Initiate a **data transfer** using the `HttpData-PULL` pattern.

In this pattern:
1. Consumer requests a transfer.
2. Provider provisions an **EDR (Endpoint Data Reference)** — a short-lived access token.
3. Consumer uses the EDR to pull data directly from the provider's Data Plane.

Transfer states: `REQUESTED` → `STARTED` → (data flowing) → `COMPLETED` or `TERMINATED`.

When state = `STARTED`, the EDR is available.

request.description:
**POST** `/v3/transferprocesses`

Start an HttpData-PULL transfer.

- `contractId` — the agreement from the negotiation
- `assetId` — must match the negotiated asset
- `transferType` — `HttpData-PULL` for consumer-pull pattern

**Response**: `IdResponse` with the `transferId`.

exec: // Guard: ensure agreement and asset are set
throw new Error('agreementId or catalogAssetId not set. Run negotiation flow (folder 6) first.');

request.description:
**GET** `/v3/transferprocesses/{id}`

Poll the transfer status. States:
- `REQUESTED` — sent to provider
- **`STARTED`** — EDR provisioned, data available ✓
- `SUSPENDED` — temporarily paused
- `COMPLETED` — finished normally
- `TERMINATED` — failed ✗

> **Auto-retries** up to 15 times when run via newman/collection runner.
> Use `--delay-request 2000` in Newman to add 2s between retries.

exec: throw new Error('transferId not set. Run "8. Initiate Transfer" first.');
pm.test('Transfer should not be TERMINATED', function () {
pm.test('Transfer should start within 15 polls', function () {
pm.expect.fail('Timed out after 15 polls, last state: ' + state);

request.description:
**POST** `/v3/transferprocesses/request`

Query all transfer processes on the consumer. Supports `QuerySpec` with pagination (`offset`/`limit`) and `filterExpression`.

Each transfer object contains:
- `@id` — transfer process ID
- `state` — current state (`REQUESTED`, `STARTED`, `COMPLETED`, `SUSPENDED`, `TERMINATED`)
- `type` — direction: `CONSUMER` or `PROVIDER`
- `assetId` — the asset being transferred
- `contractId` — the agreement authorizing this transfer
- `transferType` — `HttpData-PULL` or `HttpData-PUSH`
- `dataDestination` — (PUSH only) the target endpoint

**GET** `/v3/transferprocesses/{id}/state`

Get only the transfer state (lightweight).

**Response**: `{"state": "STARTED"}`.

exec: if (!tId) throw new Error('transferId not set. Run "8. Initiate Transfer" first.');

item.name: 9. EDR & Data Pull
item.description:
**Origin: Tractus-X customized** — `edr-api-v2` replaces upstream `edr-cache-api`. Adds auto-refresh, force-refresh, and EDR deletion not available in vanilla EDC.

After the transfer reaches `STARTED`, an **EDR (Endpoint Data Reference)** is cached.

The EDR contains:
- `endpoint` — the provider Data Plane URL to pull data from
- `authorization` — a short-lived JWT token
- `tx-auth:refreshEndpoint` — where to refresh the token
- `tx-auth:expiresIn` — token TTL in seconds (typically 300s)

> **Important**: The `endpoint` uses the Docker hostname (`provider-dp:8081`). For host access, replace with `localhost:19197`.

request.description:
**GET** `/v3/edrs/{transferProcessId}/dataaddress`

Retrieve the EDR for a given transfer. Returns:
- `endpoint` — data plane public URL
- `authorization` — Bearer token
- `tx-auth:refreshEndpoint` — refresh URL
- `tx-auth:expiresIn` — seconds until token expires

The test script automatically sets `edrEndpoint` and `edrToken` variables.

exec: // Guard: ensure transfer ID is set
throw new Error('transferId not set. Run "8. Initiate Transfer" first.');
// Rewrite Docker hostname → localhost for host access

request.description:
**GET** `<edrEndpoint>` with `Authorization: <edrToken>`

Pull the actual data from the provider's Data Plane using the EDR token.

The endpoint and token were auto-extracted from the previous step.

> This is the payoff — actual sovereign data exchange happening here!

For this demo, the data comes from jsonplaceholder.typicode.com and returns a TODO item.

exec: // Guard: ensure EDR variables are set
throw new Error('edrEndpoint or edrToken not set. Run "9. Get EDR" first.');

request.description:
**POST** `/v3/edrs/{transferProcessId}/refresh`

Refresh the EDR token. Returns a new `DataAddress` with fresh `authorization` token.

Useful when the token has expired (default TTL 300s).

**Response**: New DataAddress with refreshed token.

exec: if (!tId) throw new Error('transferId not set. Run "8. Initiate Transfer" first.');

request.description:
**DELETE** `/v3/edrs/{transferProcessId}`

Remove an EDR entry from the cache. After deletion, data can no longer be pulled.

**Response**: 204 No Content (or 200/404 for idempotent cleanup).

exec: if (!tId) throw new Error('transferId not set. Run "8. Initiate Transfer" first.');
// 404 = EDR already expired or previously deleted (acceptable in idempotent cleanup)
if (pm.response.code === 404) console.log('EDR not found — already expired or deleted.');

item.name: 10. PUSH Transfer
item.description:
**HttpData-PUSH** — the provider's data plane pushes data directly to a consumer-provided HTTP endpoint.

The consumer can specify **any HTTP endpoint** as the destination — a REST API, data ingestion service, webhook, event queue, etc. In our tests, we use a webhook service (e.g. webhook.site) as a stand-in since there is no real consumer backend.

This is the alternative to the PULL model (folders 8-9) where the consumer fetches data via EDR.

**How it works:**
1. Consumer initiates transfer with `transferType: HttpData-PUSH` and a `dataDestination` containing the consumer's endpoint URL
2. Provider's data plane fetches from the data source and POSTs it to the consumer's endpoint
3. Transfer reaches `STARTED` state once delivery begins

> **Prerequisite**: A valid `agreementId` from the negotiation flow (folder 6-7).
> Update the `webhookUrl` collection variable with your own endpoint URL.

request.description:
**POST** `/v3/transferprocesses`

Initiate an **HttpData-PUSH** transfer. Unlike PULL transfers, the provider's data plane will actively POST the data to the consumer-provided `dataDestination.baseUrl`.

The consumer can specify any HTTP endpoint — a REST API, data ingestion service, webhook, etc.

**Key differences from PULL:**
| | PULL | PUSH |
|---|---|---|
| `transferType` | `HttpData-PULL` | `HttpData-PUSH` |
| `dataDestination` | Not needed | Required — the consumer's HTTP endpoint URL |
| Data flow | Consumer fetches via EDR | Provider pushes to consumer endpoint |
| EDR created? | Yes | No |

> **Tip**: For testing, use webhook.site or a similar service to inspect the pushed data.

exec: // Guard: ensure agreement and asset are set
throw new Error('agreementId or catalogAssetId not set. Run negotiation flow (folder 6) first.');

request.description:
**GET** `/v3/transferprocesses/{id}`

Poll the PUSH transfer status.

For PUSH transfers, once state = `STARTED`, the provider has begun (or completed) pushing data to your endpoint.

> **Auto-retries** up to 15 times when run via newman/collection runner.
> Use `--delay-request 2000` in Newman to add 2s between retries.

exec: throw new Error('pushTransferId not set. Run "10. Initiate PUSH Transfer" first.');

console.log('Check your consumer endpoint for the received data!');

pm.expect.fail('Timed out after 15 polls, last state: ' + state);

item.name: 11. EDR Cache
item.description:
**Origin: Tractus-X customized** — the `POST /v3/edrs` endpoint (initiate EDR negotiation) is a TX addition that combines negotiation + transfer + EDR retrieval into a single call. Query endpoints are enhanced with TX-specific filtering.

Query the consumer's **EDR (Endpoint Data Reference) cache** to inspect and manage active data references.

EDRs are created automatically when a PULL transfer reaches `STARTED` state. Each EDR contains:
- `endpoint` — the data plane URL to fetch data from
- `authorization` — a short-lived Bearer token
- `transferProcessId` — which transfer created it

You can query EDRs by `assetId` or `agreementId` to find active data references.

> **Note**: PUSH transfers do NOT create EDRs — the provider pushes data directly to the consumer's endpoint.

request.description:
**POST** `/v3/edrs`

Initiate a combined negotiation + transfer in one step (Tractus-X convenience endpoint).

1. Starts contract negotiation
2. Auto-initiates HttpData-PULL transfer when negotiation finalizes
3. Caches the resulting EDR

Replaces the manual: Negotiate → Wait → Transfer → Wait → Get EDR flow.

Request body is same as `POST /v3/contractnegotiations`.

**Response**: `IdResponse` with negotiation ID.

exec: if (!offerId || !catAsset) throw new Error('offerId/catalogAssetId not set. Run "5. Request Filtered Catalog" first.');

request.description:
**POST** `/v3/edrs/request`

Query cached EDRs filtered by asset ID.

Each EDR entry shows:
- `transferProcessId` — which transfer created it
- `agreementId` — which agreement it belongs to
- `assetId` — the asset
- `providerId` — the data provider

console.log('No EDRs found — may have been deleted in earlier step (expected).');
body.forEach(e => console.log('  - transfer: ' + e.transferProcessId + ' | agreement: ' + e.agreementId));

request.description:
**POST** `/v3/edrs/request`

Query cached EDRs filtered by agreement ID.

Useful when one agreement has multiple transfers — this returns all EDRs created under that agreement.

Filter field: `agreementId` (the contract agreement ID from folder 7).

**Response**: Array of EDR entries, each with `transferProcessId`, `assetId`, `agreementId`, and `providerId`.

console.log('No EDRs found — may have been deleted in earlier step (expected).');

item.name: 12. Transfer Lifecycle
item.description:
**Origin: Upstream EDC** — standard transfer lifecycle operations, used as-is.

Manage the transfer lifecycle — suspend, resume, terminate, and deprovision transfers.

- **Suspend/Resume** — demonstrated on the PULL transfer (stays in STARTED state)
- **Terminate/Deprovision** — demonstrated on the PULL transfer after resume

> **Note**: PUSH transfers to finite data sources (single HTTP endpoint) reach COMPLETED almost immediately,
> so they cannot be suspended. The PULL transfer stays in STARTED and is suitable for lifecycle demos.

request.description:
**POST** `/v3/transferprocesses/{id}/suspend`

Suspend an active PULL transfer. Pauses the transfer process.

> Uses the **PULL transfer** because it stays in STARTED state. PUSH transfers to finite data sources complete immediately and cannot be suspended.

**Response**: 204 No Content.

exec: throw new Error('transferId not set. Run "8. Initiate Transfer" first.');

request.description:
**POST** `/v3/transferprocesses/{id}/resume`

Resume a previously suspended PULL transfer. The transfer must be in `SUSPENDED` state.

> Uses the **PULL transfer** — same transfer that was suspended in the previous step.

**Response**: 204 No Content.

exec: if (!tId) throw new Error('transferId not set. Run "8. Initiate Transfer" first.');
// Wait 2s for SUSPENDED state to fully propagate

request.description:
**POST** `/v3/transferprocesses/{id}/terminate`

Permanently terminate an active PULL transfer.

- `reason` — human-readable termination reason

After termination, the EDR is invalidated and data can no longer be pulled.

**Response**: 204 No Content.

exec: throw new Error('transferId not set. Run "8. Initiate Transfer" first.');

request.description:
**POST** `/v3/transferprocesses/{id}/deprovision`

Deprovision resources for a transfer.

> **No-op in EDC 0.15.1** — deprovisioning is no longer managed by the control plane. Exists for backward compatibility.

**Response**: 204 No Content.

exec: if (!tId) throw new Error('transferId not set. Run "8. Initiate Transfer" first.');

item.name: 14. Business Partner Groups (TX Extension)
item.description:
**Origin: Tractus-X added** — entirely new API from `edc-extensions/bpn-validation`. Not in upstream EDC. Enables group-based access control for the `BusinessPartnerGroup` policy constraint.

Manage **BPN-to-group mappings** — a Tractus-X extension.

BPN groups are used by the `BusinessPartnerGroup` policy constraint to restrict access based on group membership.

> **API Path**: `/v3/business-partner-groups`
> **Extension**: `edc-extensions/bpn-validation`

request.description:
**POST** `/v3/business-partner-groups`

Assign a BPN to one or more groups.

**Response**: 204 No Content.

**GET** `/v3/business-partner-groups/{bpn}`

Retrieve group memberships for a BPN.

**Response**: 200 with object containing `bpn` and `groups` array.

**GET** `/v3/business-partner-groups/group/{group}`

Retrieve all BPNs in a specific group.

**Response**: 200 with array of BPN entries.

**GET** `/v3/business-partner-groups/groups`

Retrieve all defined groups.

**Response**: Array of group names.

**PUT** `/v3/business-partner-groups`

Update group memberships for a BPN. Replaces the entire group list.

**Response**: 204 No Content.

**DELETE** `/v3/business-partner-groups/{bpn}`

Remove a BPN's group mappings.

**Response**: 204 No Content.

item.name: 15. Agreement Retirements (TX Extension)
item.description:
**Origin: Tractus-X added** — entirely new API from `edc-extensions/agreements`. Not in upstream EDC. Adds agreement retirement/reactivation for compliance lifecycle management.

Manage **contract agreement retirements** — a Tractus-X extension.

Retiring makes an agreement inactive without deleting it. Retired agreements cannot be used for new transfers but remain in the audit trail. They can be reactivated later.

> **API Path**: `/v3/contractagreements/retirements`
> **Extension**: `edc-extensions/agreements`

request.description:
**POST** `/v3/contractagreements/retirements`

Retire a contract agreement. Cannot be used for new transfers after retirement.

**Response**: 204 No Content.

> This is a Tractus-X extension — upstream EDC has no retirement concept.

exec: if (!agrId) throw new Error('agreementId not set. Run negotiation flow (folder 6) first.');

request.description:
**POST** `/v3/contractagreements/retirements/request`

Query all retired agreements. Supports `QuerySpec` pagination.

**Response**: Array of retired agreement objects.

**DELETE** `/v3/contractagreements/retirements/{agreementId}`

Reactivate a retired agreement. After reactivation it can be used for transfers again.

**Response**: 204 No Content.

exec: if (!agrId) throw new Error('agreementId not set.');

item.name: 16. Connector Discovery (TX v4alpha)
item.description:
**Origin: Tractus-X added** — entirely new v4alpha API from `edc-extensions/connector-discovery`. Not in upstream EDC. Resolves connector endpoints from participant DIDs/BPNs using BDRS.

Discover connector endpoints and DSP version parameters — a Tractus-X **v4alpha** (experimental) extension.

> **API Path**: `/v4alpha/connectordiscovery`
> **Extension**: `edc-extensions/connector-discovery`

request.description:
**POST** `/v4alpha/connectordiscovery/dspversionparams`

Query the DSP version parameters for a known connector endpoint.

Unlike the `/connectors` endpoint which discovers endpoints from a DID document,
this endpoint checks what DSP protocol versions a specific endpoint supports
by hitting its `/.well-known/dspace-version` endpoint.

**Response**: 200 with `protocol`, `counterPartyAddress`, and `counterPartyId`.

**POST** `/v4alpha/connectordiscovery/connectors`

Consumer discovers provider's connector DSP endpoints via BPN.

The full resolution chain: BPN → DID (via BDRS) → DID document → `DataService` entries → `.well-known/dspace-version` → DSP version parameters.

**Response**: 200 with array of connector endpoint records including `protocol`, `counterPartyAddress`, and `counterPartyId`.

> This v4alpha endpoint is **experimental** and may change in future EDC versions.

item.name: 17. Cleanup (Provider)
item.description:
Remove demo resources created during the walkthrough.

**Order matters** — delete in reverse dependency order:
1. Negotiation record (if deletable)
2. Contract Definition (depends on policies + asset)
3. Policy Definitions
4. Asset

> Contract agreements and transfers cannot be deleted — they are immutable audit records.

request.description:
**DELETE** `/v3/contractnegotiations/{id}`

Delete a negotiation record. Only **terminated** negotiations can be deleted — finalized negotiations are permanent audit records.

Deletes the sacrificial negotiation that was terminated in step 6.

**Response**: 204 No Content.

exec: if (!negId) throw new Error('sacrificialNegotiationId not set. Run "6. Terminate Negotiation" first.');

request.description:
**DELETE** `/v3/contractdefinitions/{id}`

Remove the contract definition. The asset will no longer appear in the catalog for new negotiations.

**Response**: 204 No Content.

**DELETE** `/v3/policydefinitions/{id}`

Remove the access policy.

The access policy controls catalog visibility — once deleted, the associated contract definition can no longer authorize catalog access.

> **Prerequisite**: Delete the contract definition first (it references this policy). Deleting a policy still referenced by a contract definition may fail.

**Expected**: 204 No Content (or 404 if already deleted).

**DELETE** `/v3/policydefinitions/{id}`

Remove the contract policy.

The contract policy defines the ODRL constraints that consumers must satisfy during negotiation — once deleted, no new negotiations can reference it.

> **Prerequisite**: Delete the contract definition first.

**Expected**: 204 No Content (or 404 if already deleted).

**DELETE** `/v3/assets/{id}`

Attempt to delete the demo asset.

**Expected: 409 Conflict** — In a full E2E flow, the asset has active contract agreements that reference it. EDC enforces referential integrity: assets with agreements cannot be deleted. This is by-design — agreements are immutable audit records, and their referenced assets must be preserved.

> To fully remove an asset, all referencing agreements would need to be removed first — which EDC does not support (by design).

exec: console.log('Asset has active agreements — cannot delete (expected).');
// Reset dynamic variables for next run
